Prerequisites: BIOL 1300 and EXSS 2050 Note: EXSS majors only.

Course: EXSS 3001 — Assessments for Strength and Conditioning (1 credits)
Offered: Spring The course will cover pertinent fitness assessments for competitive and elite athletes in the field of strength and conditioning. Assessments will cover measurements of muscular fitness, power, agility, coordination, body composition and range of motion. Note: EXSS majors only. Pre/Concurrent: EXSS 3120
Prerequisites: None listed

Course: BIOL 2004 — Human Anatomy and Physiology I (4 credits)
Offered: Fall The course provides a detailed examination of the structure and function of the human body on the cellular, tissue, and organ system levels. Cellular metabolism, histology, and the skeletal, muscular, nervous, endocrine systems and special senses are examined. Laboratory complements lecture topics. Prerequisite(s): BIOL 1800 , BIOL 1900 , and CHEM 1300 Note: Credit is granted only for Biology 2004/2005 or Biology 1200/1300 or BIOL 2420.
Prerequisites: BIOL 1800 , BIOL 1900 , and CHEM 1300

Course: BIOL 2005 — Human Anatomy and Physiology II (4 credits)
Offered: Spring This course is a continuation of BIOL 2004 (Human Anatomy and Physiology I) . The following systems are covered: circulatory, respiratory, digestive, lymphatic, digestive excretory, and reproductive. The laboratory complements lecture topics. Prerequisite(s): BIOL 1800 , BIOL 1900 , BIOL 2004 , and CHEM 1300 Note: Credit is granted only for Biology 2004/2005 or Biology 1200/1300 or BIOL 2420. Back to Top | Print-Friendly Page (opens a new window) Facebook this Page (opens a new window) Tweet this Page (opens a new window) Add to Portfolio (opens a new window)
Prerequisites: BIOL 1800 , BIOL 1900 , BIOL 2004 , and CHEM 1300 Note: Credit is granted only for Biology 2004/2005 or Biology 1200/1300 or BIOL 2420.

Course: EXSS 3011 — Practicum in Strength and Conditioning I-a (1 credits)
Offered: Spring The Practicum course is designed to expose students to the profession of strength and conditioning and to provide experience through the observation of specific protocols of exercise and fitness assessment. Students will be engaged in strength and conditioning activities within the Fitchburg State University Strength and Conditioning facility. Note: EXSS majors only. Pre/Concurrent: EXSS 3120
Prerequisites: None listed

Course: EXSS 3012 — Practicum in Strength and Conditioning I-b (1 credits)
Offered: Fall The Practicum course is designed to expose students to the profession of strength and conditioning and to provide experience through the observation of specific protocols of exercise and fitness assessment. Students will be engaged in strength and conditioning activities within the Fitchburg State University Strength and Conditioning facility. Note: EXSS majors only. Pre/Concurrent: EXSS 3120
Prerequisites: None listed

Course: EXSS 4002 — Practicum in Strength and Conditioning II-a (2 credits)
Offered: Spring The Practicum course is designed to expose students to the profession of strength and conditioning and to provide experience in supervising, coaching, and instructing athletes on specific protocols of exercise and fitness assessment. Students will be engaged in strength and conditioning activities within the Fitchburg State University Strength and Conditioning facility. Prerequisite(s): EXSS 3011 and EXSS 3012 Note: EXSS majors only.
Prerequisites: EXSS 3011 and EXSS 3012

Course: EXSS 4003 — Practicum in Strength and Conditioning II-b (2 credits)
Offered: Fall The Practicum course is designed to expose students to the profession of strength and conditioning and to provide experience in supervising, coaching, and instructing athletes on specific protocols of exercise and fitness assessment. Students will be engaged in strength and conditioning activities within the Fitchburg State University Strength and Conditioning facility. Prerequisite(s): EXSS 3011 and EXSS 3012 Note: EXSS majors only.
Prerequisites: EXSS 3011 and EXSS 3012

Course: GAME 2001 — Games and Arts in Japan (3 credits)
Offered: every other year This course explores contemporary arts and culture in Japan with an emphasis on the three pillars of Japan’s modern culture–video games, Anime and Manga. These contemporary art forms will be examined in the context of the country’s tradition of design, visual arts and culture in order to gain a better understanding of Japanese Game Art Aesthetics. The course will culminate in a study abroad trip to Japan.
Prerequisites: None listed

Course: IDIS 3004 — Interdisciplinary Studies Research Seminar (3 credits)
Offered: every semester This course introduces Interdisciplinary Studies as a means of solving complex problems by integrating the insights of multiple disciplines. Students develop the conceptual and procedural skills essential to producing excellent interdisciplinary research. Conceptual skills include the capacity to deal with complexity, understand multiple viewpoints, and connect ideas. Procedural skills include the ability to develop research questions, identify relevant disciplines, search diverse literatures, and evaluate disciplinary insights. The culminating assignment of the course requires students to write a proposal for the interdisciplinary research project or creative scholarly activity based on their own disciplinary interests and experience, which they will implement in IDIS 4004 . Prerequisite(s): Junior Standing
Prerequisites: Junior Standing

Course: GEOG 3004 — GIS for Criminal Justice (3 credits)
Offered: Spring The course will introduce students to the theory and application of crime mapping and analysis in a GIS (Geographic Information System) environment. Students will learn the criminological theories that support crime analysis and place-based policing. Students will also learn the basic skills of using GIS to create crime maps and to analyze crime patterns. Cross-listed with CJ 3004 Prerequisite(s): CJ 1000 or GEOG 2400 Meets General Education Requirement(s): IHIP, PL
Prerequisites: CJ 1000 or GEOG 2400

Course: CJ 3004 — GIS for Criminal Justice (3 credits)
Offered: Spring The course will introduce students to the theory and application of crime mapping and analysis in a GIS (Geographic Information System) environment. Students will learn the criminological theories that support crime analysis and place-based policing. Students will also learn the basic skills of using GIS to create crime maps and to analyze crime patterns. Cross-listed with GEOG 3004 Prerequisite(s): CJ 1000 or GEOG 2400 Meets General Education Requirement(s): IHIP, PL
Prerequisites: CJ 1000 or GEOG 2400

Course: CSC 3011 — Data Modeling and Database Design (3 credits)
Offered: Spring In this course, students will learn how to create data models from business requirements and transform them into logical and physical database designs and study the evolution and advantages of various database models. It teaches students how to design relational databases by applying ERD modeling and normalization techniques. Students will work with a database engine and the SQL language to create applications using a high-level language. Prerequisite(s): CSC 2560
Prerequisites: CSC 2560

Course: SPED 3008 — Applied Behavior Analysis Across Settings (3 credits)
Offered: every semester This course provides students with practical experience regarding the principles and techniques of Applied Behavior Analysis. Through a combination of didactic and experiential activities, students will gain experience and skill in measurement and assessment of behavior; techniques for skill acquisition and behavior reduction; expectations and protocols for documentation and reporting; and professional conduct and scope of practice. Prerequisite(s): EDUC 1006
Prerequisites: EDUC 1006

Course: EDUC 4003 — Educational Studies Internship (12 credits)
Offered: every semester This internship is designed to prepare students for a career in one of a variety of non-traditional educational environments. Students are required to fulfill 375 hours (approximately 25 hours per week) of field-based activities in a business or nonprofit organization providing educational services under the direction of a field-based supervisor and a faculty supervisor. Prerequisite(s): Approval from Education Chair
Prerequisites: Approval from Education Chair

Course: ECON 2005 — Game Theory (3 credits)
Prerequisites: None listed

Course: POLS 2202 — The American Presidency (3 credits)
Offered: every other year This course examines the origins and development of the American presidency from the eighteenth century to the present. It examines the executive branch of government and the development of the office in historical context. This enables us to examine the executive branch of government as it evolved in relation to the judicial and legislative branches of government in particular eras, to consider the role that contingency and individuals have had shaping the office, and to measure the evolution of the Presidency in times of war, amidst economic transformations, and in relation to public opinion and the media. Cross-listed with HIST 2202 Prerequisite(s): HIST 1000 or HIST 1100 or HIST 1400 or HIST 1500 or POLS 1000 Meets General Education Requirement(s): AIA, CV, IHIP
Prerequisites: HIST 1000 or HIST 1100 or HIST 1400 or HIST 1500 or POLS 1000

Course: MATH 3003 — Advanced Statistics (3 credits)
Offered: every other year This course is a natural extension to an introductory statistics course. Topics covered will include regression (and least square analysis), parameter estimation and confidence intervals, simulation of discrete and continuous distributions using Monte Carlo methods, Markov Chains, Bayesian analysis, analysis of variance and simulation. The course will employ statistical software to explore the listed topics. Prerequisite(s): MATH 1700 or MATH 1800 or MATH 2800 or equivalent knowledge and either MATH 2200 or MATH 2300 Meets General Education Requirement(s): AIA, PL
Prerequisites: MATH 1700 or MATH 1800 or MATH 2800 or equivalent knowledge and either MATH 2200 or MATH 2300

Course: SOC 3001 — Social Research Methods: Qualitative (3 credits)
Offered: Fall The course introduces students to the research methods used to study the social world with a focus on those that are qualitative in nature. It emphasizes the connections among social theory, methodology, technique, and ethical practice. Topics covered in class include research design, literature reviews, field work, interviews, and data analysis. Prerequisite(s): SOC 1100 and sophomore, junior, or senior status
Prerequisites: SOC 1100 and sophomore, junior, or senior status

Course: SOC 3002 — Social Research Methods: Quantitative (3 credits)
Offered: Spring This course introduces students to quantitative (numerically-based) methods of studying the social world, as well as the types of statistics typically used in sociological analysis. Through taking this course, students will learn how to design and utilize basic methods and techniques of quantitative data-gathering, as well as how to understand, apply, and interpret basic social statistics. Students will use both manual and computer software-based approaches in their work. Prerequisite(s): MATH 1700 , SOC 1100 and sophomore, junior or senior status
Prerequisites: MATH 1700 , SOC 1100 and sophomore, junior or senior status

Course: ECON 3001 — Economics of Inequality (3 credits)
Offered: Spring The course provides students with main understanding of the causes and consequences of economic inequality both in the United States and globally. The course examines the effect of pay, income and wealth inequality on social welfare. Students will also acquire the knowledge of major inequality measures. Prerequisite(s): ECON 1100 and ECON 1200 Meets General Education Requirement(s): AIA, ER
Prerequisites: ECON 1100 and ECON 1200

Course: ECON 3002 — Basic Data Skills for Economics and Business (3 credits)
Offered: Fall The course aims to introduce students to the basic data skills using mainly economic data sets. Topics covered will include locating the data sources for economic data; organizing and transforming data; presenting data including charts, simple descriptive statistics, and writing a summary report in economics. Students will learn how to find, present, and analyze economic data. The class will include hands-on experience with handling economic data. Prerequisite(s): ECON 1100 or ECON 1200
Prerequisites: ECON 1100 or ECON 1200

Course: ECON 4000 — Economics Senior Seminar (3 credits)
Offered: every other year The senior seminar in the Economics major provides a capstone course where students demonstrate mastery of the skills needed to earn a Bachelors degree in Economics. Students will create a portfolio of work and engage in original research in the field of Economics. Prerequisite(s): Economics major or minor with at least junior standing and MATH 1700 or MATH 1800
Prerequisites: Economics major or minor with at least junior standing and MATH 1700 or MATH 1800

Course: GAME 3650 — Game Programming (3 credits)
Prerequisites: None listed

Course: ART 3003 — How Photography Became Art (3 credits)
Offered: every other year This course focuses on the dynamic relationship between photography and Western art. In it, we investigate how the changing relationship between art and science in the 16th century led to the development of photography in the beginning of the 19th century, and how this development in turn transformed ideas about art and visual culture in the 19th and 20th centuries. Note: this is a cultural history course, not a production course. Prerequisite(s): ENGL 1200 Meets General Education Requirement(s): FA
Prerequisites: ENGL 1200

Course: GEOG 2003 — Environmental Geology (3 credits)
Offered: every other year Environmental Geology course engages students in a science-based approach to understanding and solving environmental problems. Topics range from impact of earthquakes, volcanic eruptions and flooding on local communities, to climatic changes on a global scale and the effects of the current world population on the carrying capacity of Earth. We will use scientific principles as the baseline of our discussions, but will also consider how policy, economics, and social norms influence attitudes towards, and use of, natural resources. Prerequisite(s): Successful completion of the Quantitative Reasoning requirement. Meets General Education Requirement(s): AIA
Prerequisites: Successful completion of the Quantitative Reasoning requirement.

Course: GEOG 2080 — Environmental Policy (3 credits)
Offered: every other year Environmental Policy focuses on the science behind environmental problems and how political solutions are derived. Students in this course will review the three branches of government and their role in establishing environmental laws, discuss the history of environmental policies and laws, and investigate the role of agencies, outside organizations and influencers in environmental policy. Philosophical debates on environmental ethics, regulatory approaches and sustainable practices will also be explored. Cross-listed with POLS 2080 Prerequisite(s): ENGL 1200 or HON 1200 Meets General Education Requirement(s): AIA, CV, IHIP, PL
Prerequisites: ENGL 1200 or HON 1200

Course: POLS 2080 — Environmental Policy (3 credits)
Offered: every other year Environmental Policy focuses on the science behind environmental problems and how political solutions are derived. Students in this course will review the three branches of government and their role in establishing environmental laws, discuss the history of environmental policies and laws, and investigate the role of agencies, outside organizations and influencers in environmental policy. Philosophical debates on environmental ethics, regulatory approaches and sustainable practices will also be explored. Cross-listed with GEOG 2080 Prerequisite(s): ENGL 1200 or HON 1200 Meets General Education Requirement(s): AIA, CV, IHIP, PL
Prerequisites: ENGL 1200 or HON 1200

Course: ENGL 2040 — Introduction to Creative Writing (3 credits)
Offered: Day course offered every semester. This course serves as an introduc tion to the elements of fiction, creative nonfiction, and poetry. Students will engage in in - class writing activitie s, discuss student - produced creative writing in the form of sma ll group workshops , and receive extensive feedback from the professor. Also, students will discuss assigned readings with an emphasis on craft . Students are encouraged to take this class prior to other creative writi ng courses . This course is available to majors and non - majors. Credit not awarded for both ENGL 3500 and ENGL 2040. Prerequisite(s): ENGL 1200 or HON 1200
Prerequisites: ENGL 1200 or HON 1200

Course: ENGL 2020 — Style Studio (3 credits)
Offered: every other year This workshop-based studio course encourages students to increase their stylistic flexibility by practicing rhetorical and literary devices, copying stylistic models, and adapting and inventing unique styles. Students will analyze short written texts from a wide range of styles and genres, rewrite pieces in diverse styles, and attempt to forge the styles of the authors we read. The course helps writers break from their stylistic habits and experiment with new ways to use language and explore their voice. Prerequisite(s): ENGL 1200 or HON 1200 Meets General Education Requirement(s): AIA, PL
Prerequisites: ENGL 1200 or HON 1200

Course: ENGL 2025 — Foundations of Professional Writing (3 credits)
Offered: Fall This course provides an introduction to rhetorical theories and techniques for writers across genres and media as well as background in the history of writing and writing as technology. Students will see writing from many dimensions (as craft, communication, linguistic structure, cultural marker, historical record, and medium for language) and will engage with information on potential pathways toward professionalization. Prerequisite(s): ENGL 1200 or HON 1200 Meets General Education Requirement(s): AIA
Prerequisites: ENGL 1200 or HON 1200

Course: ENGL 2030 — News Reporting and Writing (3 credits)
Offered: Fall This course will provide students with the necessary skills and tools to effectively research and write articles for news publications. Students will learn how to evaluate newsworthy content, pitch story ideas, conduct in-depth research, interview subjects, and write news articles for various mediums. Prerequisite(s): ENGL 1200 or HON 1200 Meets General Education Requirement(s): CV
Prerequisites: ENGL 1200 or HON 1200

Course: ENGL 2003 — Latin American Literature (3 credits)
Offered: as needed This class, conducted in English, traces major cultural and political moments in Latin America, Spain and the Caribbean from the 1920s to the present. We will explore cultural products from a broad range of genres and media (fiction, non- fiction, film, music) in order to reflect upon significant artistic trends, political movements and intellectual debates of the last century: modernism and modernity, citizenship and nationhood, revolution, subalternity, dictatorship and postdictatorship, political economy, migration, and globalization. Students will be expected to contribute oral and written assignments reflecting upon these works and- through these assignments as well as active participation in class meetings- will learn to think critically about Hispanic cultural production. Prerequisite(s): ENGL 1200 OR HON 1200 Meets General Education Requirement(s): AIA, DP, LI
Prerequisites: ENGL 1200 OR HON 1200

Course: BIOL 3060 — Biochemical Techniques (3 credits)
Offered: every other year This course introduces students to the most commonly used techniques in a biochemistry lab. Students will have hands-on experience with techniques such as buffer preparation, spectrophotometry, recombinant protein expression, protein purification, column chromatography, gel electrophoresis, Western blotting, affinity purification, enzyme activity assays. Cross-listed with CHEM 3060 Prerequisite(s): CHEM 3030 / BIOL 3030 with a 1.7 or higher, OR CHEM 2000 and BIOL 2800 each with a 1.7 or higher Meets General Education Requirement(s): AIA, IHIP
Prerequisites: CHEM 3030 / BIOL 3030 with a 1.7 or higher, OR CHEM 2000 and BIOL 2800 each with a 1.7 or higher

Course: CHEM 3060 — Biochemical Techniques (3 credits)
Offered: every other year This course introduces students to the most commonly used techniques in a biochemistry lab. Students will have hands-on experience with techniques such as buffer preparation, spectrophotometry, recombinant protein expression, protein purification, column chromatography, gel electrophoresis, Western blotting, affinity purification, enzyme activity assays. Cross-listed with BIOL 3060 Prerequisite(s): CHEM 3030 / BIOL 3030 with a 1.7 or higher, OR CHEM 2000 and BIOL 2800 each with a 1.7 or higher
Prerequisites: CHEM 3030 / BIOL 3030 with a 1.7 or higher, OR CHEM 2000 and BIOL 2800 each with a 1.7 or higher

Course: CHEM 3040 — Biochemistry II (3 credits)
Offered: every other year This course can be taken in any sequence with Biochemistry I. Topics include enzyme mechanisms, pathways and regulation of carbohydrate, lipid, amino acid, and nucleic acid biosynthesis and catabolism, photosynthesis, DNA replication, transcription and translation. Cross-listed with BIOL 3040 Prerequisite(s): BIOL 1800 and CHEM 2000 Note: Students can only receive credit for BIOL 3040 OR CHEM 3040.
Prerequisites: BIOL 1800 and CHEM 2000

Course: MUSC 2320 — Concert Choir (1 credits)
Offered: every semester A mixed chorus (soprano, alto, tenor, bass), this choir prepares choral works from various periods and styles. Concert Choir is open to all students, and partially satisfies the ensemble requirement for music minors. Extra performances required. Note: May be repeated for credit 10 times. 3 such credits can be counted toward one Gen Ed designation. Meets General Education Requirement(s): AIA, DP, FA, IHIP
Prerequisites: None listed

Offered: every semester A mixed chorus (soprano, alto, tenor, bass), this choir prepares choral works from various periods and styles. Chamber Choir is by audition only, and partially satisfies the ensemble requirement for music minors. Extra performances required. Note: May be repeated for credit 10 times. 3 such credits can be counted toward one Gen Ed designation. Meets General Education Requirement(s): AIA, DP, FA, IHIP
Prerequisites: None listed

Course: GEOG 4003 — Geographic Information Systems II (3 credits)
Offered: Fall The course exposes students to wide-ranging real-world data and Geographic Information System (GIS) applications that require integration of different GIS processing steps and workflows to solve realistic problems using ArcGIS Pro. The course is hands-on and lab-based. Prerequisite(s): CSC 1500 or GEOG 2400 or GEOG 3120 or GEOG 3300 Meets General Education Requirement(s): AIA, PL
Prerequisites: CSC 1500 or GEOG 2400 or GEOG 3120 or GEOG 3300

Course: GEOG 4001 — Web GIS (3 credits)
Offered: every other year Introduction to basic concepts and applications of Web GIS, which uses web technologies such as smart mapping, Story Maps, 3D web scenes, App Studio for ArcGIS, and Operations Dashboard, to communicate geographic information in a collaborative environment. The course is hands-on and lab-based. Prerequisite(s): CSC 1500 or GEOG 2400 or GEOG 3120 or GEOG 3300 Meets General Education Requirement(s): AIA, PL
Prerequisites: CSC 1500 or GEOG 2400 or GEOG 3120 or GEOG 3300

Course: SPED 4350 — Academic Instruction for Students with Severe Disabilities (3 credits)
Prerequisites: None listed

Course: SPED 3023 — Field Experience I in Moderate Disabilities (2 credits)
Offered: every semester The Field Experience I course in Moderate Disabilities provides candidates a uniquely guided teaching and learning experience in an inclusive classroom or special education setting under the supervision of a MA licensed teacher and university faculty. Candidates plan and deliver instruction to diverse learners in one-on-one, small group, and whole class settings. Candidates enrolled in this course fulfill the hours for the Massachusetts Department of Elementary and Secondary Education pre-practicum requirements. In this 90-hour field experience, candidates will practice and demonstrate the Subject Matter Knowledge standards (SMKs) as well as the Professional Standards for Teachers (PSTs). In addition, they will implement coursework from their education methods courses aligned with this course and will receive targeted, specific feedback in areas of reinforcement and refinement. Prerequisite(s): Succesful Passage of Stage One Education Unit Review
Prerequisites: Succesful Passage of Stage One Education Unit Review

Candidates enrolled in this course fulfill the hours for the Massachusetts Department of Elementary and Secondary Education pre-practicum requirements. In this 90-hour field experience, candidates will practice and demonstrate the Subject Matter Knowledge standards (SMKs) as well as the Professional Standards for Teachers (PSTs). In addition, they will implement coursework from their education methods courses aligned with this course and will receive targeted, specific feedback in areas of reinforcement and refinement. Candidates are advised to complete Field Experience I in Severe Disabilities prior to registering for this course. Prerequisite(s): Successful Passage of Stage One Education Unit Review

Course: EDUC 2009 — The Art and Science of Education (3 credits)
Offered: every semester This course introduces learning theory as the science of education in the classroom and research-based practice as the art of teaching. Memory, prior knowledge, constructivism, motivation, assessment, and social development are explored as examples of influences on instruction. Candidates learn the theory behind strategies that engage and accommodate all students in a collaborative, positive learning environment. Note: Required for all Early Childhood Education (PreK-2), Elementary Education (1-6), Special Education: Moderate Disabilities (PreK-8) and Severe Disabilities (all levels) majors.
Prerequisites: None listed

Course: SPED 4025 — Identification and Interventions for Students with Learning Disabilities (3 credits)
Offered: every semester This course focuses on the principles of identification and instruction for students with learning disabilities and ADHD. Candidates will examine the process of eligibility for special education services from progress monitoring in Response to Intervention (RTI) to implementing and evaluating Individualized Education Programs (IEP) and 504 plans. Candidates will design and implement effective learning strategies and interventions to support students across settings including the general education classroom. Current research and major scientific advances in understanding learning disabilities in math, reading, and written expression will be explored. State required documentation and components for team determination of eligibility for specific learning disabilities will be included. Prerequisite(s): Successful Passage of Stage One Education Unit Review Note: Required for Special Education: Moderate Disabilities (PreK-8) majors. Corequisite(s): SPED 4000
Prerequisites: Successful Passage of Stage One Education Unit Review

Course: ART 3002 — Ceramics II (3 credits)
Offered: Spring Ceramics II is an intermediate-level investigation into the historical and contemporary sculptural practices using clay as the medium. Students will create a variety of sculptures, such as a portrait bust, an animal sculpture, an abstracted organic piece, and an architectural structure. An orientation to the processes of hand building will include pinching, coil building, slab building, modeling, and carving. Students will be able to take this course twice for credit. Prerequisite(s): ART 1300 or ART 1650 or ART 2004 or permission of instructor Meets General Education Requirement(s): AIA, FA

Offered: every semester This course introduces the terminology, strategies, and issues commonly encountered when identifying and addressing the needs of diverse students with disabilities. Emphasis will be placed on inclusion, diversity issues, federal and state legislative mandates pertinent to nondiscriminatory assessments, parental involvement, Individualized Education Programs, and professional practice in special education. This course serves to provide a foundation for future courses focusing on content specific instruction, accommodations and modifications. Three observations of special education services in various settings are required.

Course: PHS 3000 — Environmental Health (3 credits)
Offered: every other year Environmental health is a branch of public health that focuses on the influence of both the natural and built environment on human health. Students in this course will examine the impact of physical, chemical and biological factors on human health, including air and water quality, natural disasters, radiation exposure, indoor air pollution, food-borne diseases, soil contaminants, and climate change. Prerequisite(s): GEOG 1000 or GEOG 2003
Prerequisites: GEOG 1000 or GEOG 2003

Course: PHS 2000 — Public Health in the United States (3 credits)
Offered: Spring The course focuses on public health in contemporary American society. Emphasis is placed on underlying theories and the scientific and social bases for public health practice, plus the impact of critical public health concerns on society.

Course: CSC 1002 — Computational Thinking (3 credits)
Offered: as needed This is a breadth-first introductory course to computer science and computational thinking. This course provides a survey of computer science, computer programming, algorithms, data structures, and other areas including programming languages, object-oriented programming, graphical user interfaces, artificial intelligence, operating systems, etc. Students will learn to think computationally in solving computable problems as well as learn the basics of using variables, conditionals, and loops in creating a programmatic solution. Prerequisite(s): Passing score on Advanced Algebra and Functions Accuplacer placement exam OR successful completion of MATH 0500 [Algebraic Prep.]
Prerequisites: Passing score on Advanced Algebra and Functions Accuplacer placement exam OR successful completion of MATH 0500 [Algebraic Prep.]

Course: BIOL 4009 — Cancer Genomics (4 credits)
Offered: every other year An examination of the molecular and cellular mechanisms that contribute to the development of cancer. Therapeutic strategies for cancer treatment will be considered. In the lab, students will use bioinformatics techniques to explore the molecular changes associated with tumorigenesis. Prerequisite(s): A grade of 1.7 in BIOL 2300 and BIOL 2800 or permission of the instructor. Meets General Education Requirement(s): IHIP
Prerequisites: A grade of 1.7 in BIOL 2300 and BIOL 2800 or permission of the instructor.

Course: CMGT 4020 — Communication and Transportation Technologies (3 credits)
Offered: Spring The course studies communication and transportation systems specifically as they relate to the construction industry. Above and below ground infrastructure development for transportation, traffic management, and communication systems are addressed as part of this course. Prerequisite(s): CMGT 3035 , ENGT 2030 , and ENGT 3022
Prerequisites: CMGT 3035 , ENGT 2030 , and ENGT 3022

Course: CMGT 4025 — Construction Law and the Legal System (3 credits)
Prerequisites: None listed

Course: CSC 4100 — Ethics and Impacts of Computing Solutions (3 credits)
Offered: Spring This course covers various ethical issues and global impacts that arise as a result of increasing use of computers in contemporary society. Topics include methodology, tools and frameworks for analysis of ethical issues in Computer Science, global and local impacts and ethical issues raised in networked communications, intellectual property rights, information privacy, computer and network security, computer reliability and the ethical responsibilities of computing professionals. Prerequisite(s): Junior or Senior status Meets General Education Requirement(s): ER
Prerequisites: Junior or Senior status

Course: MUSC 3150 — Music of the Stage (3 credits)
Offered: every two years This course is a beginner’s survey of the history of opera and musical theater from opera’s origins through the present, including defining features, significant works and figures, social impact, and cultural context. Meets General Education Requirement(s): FA
Prerequisites: None listed

Course: MUSC 2401 — Concert Band (1 credits)
Offered: every semester A large instrumental ensemble studying, rehearsing and performing contemporary and traditional wind band literature. Open to all students with experience playing a band instrument. Partially satisfies the ensemble requirement for music minors. Extra performances required. Note: May be repeated for credit 10 times. 3 such credits can be counted toward one Gen Ed designation. Meets General Education Requirement(s): AIA, DP, FA, IHIP
Prerequisites: None listed

Course: MUSC 2402 — Community Orchestra (1 credits)
Offered: every semester A large instrumental ensemble studying, rehearsing and performing contemporary and traditional orchestra literature. Open to all students with experience playing an orchestral instrument. Partially satisfies the ensemble requirement for music minors. Extra performances required. Note: May be repeated for credit 10 times. 3 such credits can be counted toward one Gen Ed designation. Meets General Education Requirement(s): AIA, DP, FA, IHIP
Prerequisites: None listed

Course: EDUC 2011 — Diversity in Education (5-12) (3 credits)
Offered: Fall Students will examine issues of diversity in classrooms and schools, including practices that obstruct and support equality and neuro-diversity; diversification of the curriculum; and interpersonal communication skills. Needs of LGBTQ students and students of all cultural, ethnic, racial, religious and socio-economic backgrounds will be addressed. A pre-practicum of 10 hours is required. Meets General Education Requirement(s): DP
Prerequisites: None listed

Course: PSY 2025 — Cultural Psychology (3 credits)
Offered: every spring This course will examine theories and research on the role of culture in human development and psychological functioning. Topics will include: research methods used in cross-cultural research; the effects of culture on foundational areas of psychology (e.g., cognition, learning, development, emotion, motivation, self & identity, personality, language, and social/interpersonal behavior, and health); and applications to personal and professional domains. Prerequisite(s): PSY 1100 or by permission of instructor Note: Note: This course cannot be taken if you have already taken PSY 3020. Meets General Education Requirement(s): AIA
Prerequisites: PSY 1100 or by permission of instructor

Course: PSY 2020 — Professional Orientation to Psychological Science (3 credits)
Offered: Fall This course is designed to provide students with an overview of the Psychological Science major, skills and resources needed to be successful within the major/career, and career options for majors. Topics will include the Psychological Science major requirements, critical and scientific thinking, locating and interpreting scientific sources of information, scientific writing and APA-style, ethics and professionalism, subfields and career opportunities in psychology, and developing a career plan. Note: Restricted to Psychological Science majors only.
Prerequisites: None listed

Course: ENGL 2765 — LGBTQ Issues & Literature (3 credits)
Offered: every other year This course explores lesbian, gay, bisexual, and transgender (LGBT) issues in society and literature. We examine the historical and current construction of gender and identity. Students interpret fiction, nonfiction, and poetry using multiple perspectives; additionally, they interpret contemporary cultural texts, such as speech, art, advertising, and film. Prerequisite(s): ENGL 1200 or HON 1200 Meets General Education Requirement(s): AIA, DP, LI
Prerequisites: ENGL 1200 or HON 1200

Course: EDUC 2012 — Teaching the Adolescent Learner (5-12) (3 credits)
Offered: Sping Students will examine adolescents’ biological, cognitive, and psychological development. Teaching candidates will apply these understandings to specific and practical approaches to designing, implementing, and assessing various learning objectives, activities, and assessments, developing positive relationships with students, and creating and maintaining well-organized classrooms conducive to emotional safety. A pre-practicum of 10 hours is required.
Prerequisites: None listed

Course: MATH 3010 — Methods of Teaching Mathematics (5-12) I (3 credits)
Offered: Spring This is the first of two methods courses and will focus on content pedagogy of mathematics at the middle and secondary level. Teacher candidates design and implement standards-based instruction and assessments that are aligned with best practices for teaching and learning in mathematics. Special emphasis is placed on employing a variety of instructional practices and classroom management strategies that provide learning opportunities for diverse student populations and enhance multicultural pluralism. Teacher candidates will gain knowledge in using technology to facilitate teaching and learning based on the needs of diverse learners and across varied subject areas. A pre-practicum of 25 hours is required. Students will arrange pre-practicum hours on their own or through the support of the Placement Coordinator. An observation by the Supervising Practitioner, and the accompanying Pre-Practicum Targeted Feedback Form are required. Prerequisite(s): EDUC 2011 , EDUC 2012 , MATH 2400 , and SPED 3800
Prerequisites: EDUC 2011 , EDUC 2012 , MATH 2400 , and SPED 3800

Course: CHEM 3015 — Methods of Teaching Chemistry (8-12) I (3 credits)
Offered: Spring This is the first of two methods courses and will focus on content pedagogy of science (including general science, biology, and chemistry) at the middle and secondary level. Teacher candidates design and implement standards-based instruction and assessments that are aligned with best practices for teaching and learning in science. Special emphasis is placed on employing a variety of instructional practices and classroom management strategies that provide learning opportunities for diverse student populations and enhance multicultural pluralism. Disciplinary literacy is particularly emphasized to enhance reading, writing, speaking, and listening in science. A pre-practicum of 25 hours is required. Students will arrange pre-practicum hours on their own or through the support of the Placement Coordinator. An observation by the Supervising Practitioner, and the accompanying Pre-Practicum Targeted Feedback Form are required. Cross-listed with BIOL 3015 and SCI 3015 Prerequisite(s): BIOL 1800 , EDUC 2011 , EDUC 2012 , SPED 3800 , and either CHEM 1000 or CHEM 1300
Prerequisites: BIOL 1800 , EDUC 2011 , EDUC 2012 , SPED 3800 , and either CHEM 1000 or CHEM 1300

Course: ENGL 3082 — Methods of Teaching English (5-12) I (3 credits)
Prerequisites: None listed

Course: HIST 3010 — Methods of Teaching History (5-12) I (3 credits)
Offered: Spring This is the first of two methods courses and will focus on content pedagogy of history at the middle and secondary level. Teacher candidates design and implement standards-based instruction and assessments that are aligned with best practices for teaching and learning in history. Special emphasis is placed on employing a variety of instructional practices and classroom management strategies that provide learning opportunities for diverse student populations and enhance multicultural pluralism. Disciplinary literacy is particularly emphasized to enhance reading, writing, speaking, and listening in history. A pre-practicum of 25 hours is required. Students will arrange pre-practicum hours on their own or through the support of the Placement Coordinator. An observation by the Supervising Practitioner, and the accompanying Pre-Practicum Targeted Feedback Form are required. Prerequisite(s): HIST 2000 and SPED 3800
Prerequisites: HIST 2000 and SPED 3800

Course: ENGT 3300 — Methods of Teaching Technology/Engineering Education (5-12) I (3 credits)
Offered: Spring This is the first of two methods courses and will focus on content pedagogy of engineering/technology at the middle and secondary level. Teacher candidates design and implement standards-based instruction and assessments that are aligned with best practices for teaching and learning in engineering/technology. Special emphasis is placed on employing a variety of instructional practices and classroom management strategies that provide learning opportunities for diverse student populations and enhance multicultural pluralism. Disciplinary literacy is particularly emphasized to enhance reading, writing, speaking, and listening in technology / engineering education. A pre-practicum of 25 hours is required. Prerequisite(s): EDUC 2011 , EDUC 2012 , and SPED 3800 Note: Students pursuing initial licensure will be required to complete two ESE required gateway tasks in this course. Candidates must pass the gateway tasks to continue within the licensure concentration.
Prerequisites: EDUC 2011 , EDUC 2012 , and SPED 3800

Course: MATH 4020 — Practicum in Middle School (5-8) Mathematics I (4.5 credits)
Offered: every semester Practicum I is an 8-week student teaching experience in the role of teaching in the middle school setting. Teacher candidates complete the practicum under the direction of a supervising practitioner who is certified in the area of licensure sought by the candidate and under the guidance of the university supervisor. The purpose of the practicum is to ensure candidates are ready to teach in their content area and make an impact with students from the beginning of their experience. Targeted and specific feedback on observations of candidates, measurement of candidate impact on student learning, student feedback on candidates, and candidate artifacts will be used to improve candidate practice on key indicators of all the Professional Standards for Teachers (PSTs) and assess candidate practice on the Candidate Performance of Assessment (CAP) rubric. This process will culminate in the development of Professional Practice Goals for the candidate’s next steps. Prerequisite(s): EDUC 3122 and Successful passage of Education Unit Stage II review Corequisite(s): MATH 4012
Prerequisites: EDUC 3122 and Successful passage of Education Unit Stage II review

Course: MATH 4030 — Practicum in Middle School (5-8) Mathematics II (4.5 credits)
Offered: every semester Practicum II is an 8-week student teaching experience in the role of teaching in the middle school setting. Teacher candidates complete the practicum under the direction of a supervising practitioner who is certified in the area of licensure sought by the candidate and under the guidance of the university supervisor. The purpose of the practicum is to ensure candidates are ready to teach in their content area and make impact with students on day one in a classroom. Targeted and specific feedback on observations of candidates, measurement of candidate impact on student learning, student feedback on candidates, and candidate artifacts will be used to improve candidate practice on key indicators of all the Professional Standards for Teachers (PSTs) and assess candidate practice on the Candidate Performance of Assessment (CAP) rubric. This process will culminate in the development of Professional Practice Goals for the candidate’s next steps. Prerequisite(s): EDUC 3122 and Successful Passage of Education Unit Stage II Review Corequisite(s): MATH 4012
Prerequisites: EDUC 3122 and Successful Passage of Education Unit Stage II Review

Course: ENGT 4850 — Methods in Teaching Technology/Engineering Education (5-12) II (3 credits)
Offered: Spring This is the second part of the methods course that focuses on Technology / Engineering Education pedagogy at the middle and secondary level. Teacher candidates continue designing and implementing standards-based instruction and assessments that are aligned with best practices for teaching and learning in Technology/Engineering Education. Special emphasis is placed on employing a variety of instructional practices and classroom management strategies that provide learning opportunities for diverse student populations and enhance multicultural pluralism. Moreover, teacher candidates will gain knowledge in using technology to facilitate teaching and learning appropriate for the needs of diverse learners and across varied subject areas. This course also addresses select Technology/Engineering Education subject matter knowledge (5-12) required by ESE. A pre-practicum of 25 hours is required. Prerequisite(s): ENGT 3300
Prerequisites: ENGT 3300

Course: COMM 3207 — Data & Society (3 credits)
Offered: every other year This class explores the uses of data in Communications Media, including tailoring professional communication advertising campaigns, green-lighting film productions, creating profitable micro-transaction mechanisms in video games, and more. How is data leveraged to form arguments about society, make decisions, and generate profits? Through hands-on projects, students will analyze ethical challenges related to data visualization, algorithms, privacy, citizen and employee surveillance, and more. Meets General Education Requirement(s): CV, ER
Prerequisites: None listed

Course: COMM 3205 — Critical Making (3 credits)
Prerequisites: None listed

Course: COMM 3206 — Creative Coding (3 credits)
Offered: every other year Creative coding employs computer programming as a means of creative expression using algorithmic and generative art, data visualization, and storytelling techniques for applied projects. These skills are learned in the context of critical and cultural media studies and the digital humanities. No prior programming experience is needed; students will learn basic coding. Meets General Education Requirement(s): PL
Prerequisites: None listed

Course: DMI 4800 — Digital Media Innovation Capstone Fieldwork (3 credits)
Offered: Spring The capstone course allows students to work either individually or as a group, synthesizing and applying what they have learned in their earlier Digital Media Innovation coursework as part of an ambitious semester-long project in collaboration with community partners. Cross-listed with ENGL 4800 Prerequisite(s): DMI 4799 or ENGL 4799 Note: Credit can only be awarded for one of ENGL 4800 or COMM 4800 or DMI 4800. This course is restricted to students in the Digital Media Innovation program.
Prerequisites: DMI 4799 or ENGL 4799

Course: ENGL 4800 — Digital Media Innovation Capstone Fieldwork (3 credits)
Offered: Spring The capstone course allows students to work either individually or as a group, synthesizing and applying what they have learned in their earlier Digital Media Innovation coursework as part of an ambitious semester-long project in collaboration with community partners. Cross-listed with DMI 4800 Prerequisite(s): Will require project proposal approval by instructor
Prerequisites: Will require project proposal approval by instructor

Course: ENGL 3600 — Language, Literature, and the Digital Humanities (3 credits)
Offered: every other year This course immerses students in digital humanities and its application in literary study by actively contributing to or collaboratively building new DH projects on significant literary works, authors, genres, or periods. While students may be using existing data sets and materials, the focus of the course will be on building and not simply using data or digital works that augment how we understand literature in a digital age. Project methods and topics will differ depending on the instructor. Potential methods employed may include distant reading strategies and quantitative analysis of textual material, computational linguistics, digital archive management, or text encoding for digital edition publication. Prerequisite(s): ENGL 1200 or HON 1200 Meets General Education Requirement(s): PL
Prerequisites: ENGL 1200 or HON 1200

Course: GEOG 2006 — Water Resources and Society (3 credits)
Offered: every other year This course provides an overview of global water resources and how society interacts with water. The course will first provide a basic understanding of the properties of water and how water moves throughout the hydrologic cycle on a local and global scale. The second part of the course will examine human relationships with water with specific areas of focus including, but not limited to, flooding, agricultural use of water, water scarcity, water law, conflicts over water resources, and water contamination. Prerequisite(s): GEOG 1000 Meets General Education Requirement(s): AIA
Prerequisites: GEOG 1000

Course: ENGL 2325 — Introduction to Digital Humanities (3 credits)
Offered: every other year This course provides an overview of how digital technologies have augmented humanistic inquiry. Students will especially consider the challenges of preservation and ethical data use and structuring while they explore and build digital resources. Core outcomes of the course will be conducting archival research, digitization and metadata creation, digital textual analysis, textual markup, and web and document production and design. Each semester course projects will focus on a major concept or collection (i.e. gendered language in 1970s newspapers and magazines or mental health in the social media landscape). Cross-listed with COMM 2225 Meets General Education Requirement(s): PL
Prerequisites: None listed

Course: COMM 2225 — Introduction to Digital Humanities (3 credits)
Offered: every other year This course provides an overview of how digital technologies have augmented humanistic inquiry. Students will especially consider the challenges of preservation and ethical data use and structuring while they explore and build digital resources. Core outcomes of the course will be conducting archival research, digitization and metadata creation, digital textual analysis, textual markup, and web and document production and design. Each semester course projects will focus on a major concept or collection (i.e. gendered language in 1970s newspapers and magazines or mental health in the social media landscape). Cross-listed with ENGL 2325 Meets General Education Requirement(s): PL
Prerequisites: None listed

Course: COMM 3208 — Rhetoric in Greece (3 credits)
Offered: every other year This course explores rhetoric’s origin and evolution in classical Greece through two primary lenses: the relationship between rhetoric and democracy and the contrasting understanding of truth in Sophistic rhetoric and Socratic philosophy. Students begin by investigating the transition from orality to literacy in Ancient Greece before moving on to explore how Plato’s critique of rhetoric still impacts the field of communications media today. On a spring break trip, students explore historical locations in Greece associated with these debates in order to develop a deeper appreciation of their historical and cultural context. Finally, students will explore how the Greek intellectual tradition informs contemporary rhetorical and democratic practices, with an emphasis on fake news and post-truth. Meets General Education Requirement(s): AIA, CV, IHIP
Prerequisites: None listed

Course: PHS 4050 — Public Health Strategies (3 credits)
Offered: every other year This course provides an examination of public health theory. It addresses public health planning and response from various perspectives, including public health infrastructure, communication, and health policy and legislation. The course applies theory-based concepts and methods of health promotion interventions, as well as program planning frameworks and needs assessments. Consideration of cultural, ethical and social factors during program planning are also discussed. Prerequisite(s): PHS 3050
Prerequisites: PHS 3050

Course: BIOL 3007 — Virology (3 credits)
Offered: every other year This course introduces the study of viruses. Virus structure, classification, and replication strategies will be discussed. Infection of host cells, host immune responses, anti-viral drugs, and vaccine strategies will be examined. Viruses that infect humans will be emphasized. The use of viruses in biotechnology will also be discussed. Prerequisite(s): BIOL 2800 or permission of the instructor
Prerequisites: BIOL 2800 or permission of the instructor

Course: CHEM 3003 — Environmental Chemistry (3 credits)
Offered: every other year This course will use fundamental principles of chemistry to examine the chemistry of air, water, and pollutants, and how human activities affect this chemistry. We will study the sources, effects, and fates of chemical compounds in the environment. Prerequisite(s): CHEM 1400 Meets General Education Requirement(s): AIA
Prerequisites: CHEM 1400

Course: ART 2050 — Public Art (3 credits)
Offered: every other year This course examines the role of art in public spaces. We analyze how public art visually expresses the diverse identities, stories, and cultures of the communities who create and view it. First, we consider public art from ancient historical periods through the nineteenth century. Second, we look at public art in our local communities throughout Massachusetts. Third, we study contemporary public art around the world. Meets General Education Requirement(s): DP, FA
Prerequisites: None listed

Course: SPAN 2300 — Medical Spanish (3 credits)
Offered: Fall In SPAN 2300, students will develop Spanish skills to communicate in medical emergencies. The course is designed for healthcare providers or individuals needing medical terminology skills and intercultural communication for medical purposes in Spanish. The course focus on listening, speaking, and writing practices set around different medical settings. Prerequisite(s): SPAN 1000 or permission of instructor Meets General Education Requirement(s): AIA, DP
Prerequisites: SPAN 1000 or permission of instructor

Course: POLS 2040 — Poverty and Inequality in the Developing World (3 credits)
Offered: every other year This course is an introduction to the developing world—the nations of Africa, Asia, and Latin America. Through its focus upon the two themes of Poverty and Inequality, students will learn about the developing world—its history, politics, economics, societies, and cultures. Cross-listed with IDIS 2040 Meets General Education Requirement(s): CV, DP
Prerequisites: None listed

Course: IDIS 2040 — Poverty and Inequality in the Developing World (3 credits)
Offered: every other year. This course is an introduction to the developing world—the nations of Africa, Asia, and Latin America. Through its focus upon the two themes of Poverty and Inequality, students will learn about the developing world—its history, politics, economics, societies, and cultures. Cross-listed with POLS 2040 Meets General Education Requirement(s): CV, DP
Prerequisites: None listed

Course: COMM 4970 — Design Studio and Practice (6 credits)
Offered: Spring This course investigates common ground between theory and practice, emphasizing life-long skills of research, ideation, and critical thinking to meet the expectations of a changing multidisciplinary and global profession. Students refine and self-assess their work to identify strengths, weaknesses, and areas of design they are passionate about. Students develop a resume, assemble their portfolio, write a design statement, and develop a personalized project informed by research and theory for inclusion in their portfolio. Topics relating to design practice include client relations, budgeting, contract and brief writing, and time management. Students explore opportunities for graduate study. Prerequisite(s): COMM 3950 Meets General Education Requirement(s): AIA, IHIP
Prerequisites: COMM 3950

Course: MUSC 3000 — Fundamentals of Conducting (3 credits)
Offered: Offered every spring semester. This course is designed to introduce students to conducting music ensembles. Skills taught will include but are not limited to basic conducting patterns, score reading, analytical techniques, and rehearsal techniques. Students will participate as both conductor and member of the ensemble. Lab hours outside of class may be required. Prerequisite(s): MUSC 1001 ; MUSC 2200 or MUSC 2240 ; and one of the following: MUSC 2320 , MUSC 2330 , MUSC 2400 , MUSC 2401 , MUSC 2402
Prerequisites: MUSC 1001 ; MUSC 2200 or MUSC 2240 ; and one of the following: MUSC 2320 , MUSC 2330 , MUSC 2400 , MUSC 2401 , MUSC 2402

Course: MUSC 2140 — Instrumental Skills (3 credits)
Offered: every other Fall This is a course in the fundamentals of playing a musical instrument of the concert band or orchestra (woodwinds, brass, percussion, strings) in a class situation. It is intended for students who have no previous experience and wish to learn how to play an instrument of the orchestra or band prior to enrolling in performance ensembles and /or applied lessons. Note: Open to all students. May be repeated once for credit. Meets General Education Requirement(s): FA
Prerequisites: None listed

Course: ART 2025 — Drawing the Figure in Action (3 credits)
Offered: Fall This class will focus exclusively on the human figure: proportions, anatomy, gesture, and form. We will explore the figure in a variety of ways, including creating a posable 3D wire armature as well as drawing in pencil, charcoal, and ink wash. For students wishing to develop their ability to draw and animate the figure realistically for Game Design, Graphic Design, or Drawing and Painting, this course will give you the intense practice needed to build confidence and skill. Prerequisite(s): ART 1400 or permission from instructor Meets General Education Requirement(s): FA
Prerequisites: ART 1400 or permission from instructor

Course: DMI 4799 — Digital Media Innovation Capstone (3 credits)
Offered: Fall In this capstone course, students will collaborate with one another, a faculty member, and a community partner to plan a final, community-engaged capstone project that will be completed in a subsequent semester. Cross-listed with ENGL 4799 Prerequisite(s): Completion of all other Digital Media Innovation major requirements. Note: Credit can only be awarded for one of COMM 4799 or DMI 4799 or ENGL 4799. This course is restricted to students in the Digital Media Innovation program.
Prerequisites: Completion of all other Digital Media Innovation major requirements.

Course: ENGL 4799 — Digital Media Innovation Capstone (3 credits)
Offered: Fall In this capstone course, students will collaborate with one another, a faculty member, and a community partner to plan a final, community-engaged capstone project that will be completed in a subsequent semester. Cross-listed with DMI 4799 Prerequisite(s): Completion of all other Digital Media Innovation major requirements
Prerequisites: Completion of all other Digital Media Innovation major requirements

Course: GEOG 4880 — Teaching Practicum in Geography (1-3 credits)
Offered: as needed Students assist an individual faculty member in the teaching of GEOG course(s). Responsibilities vary with the course. The Teaching Practicum is offered by invitation of faculty only. Prerequisite(s): Permission of instructor and GEOG 1000 or GEOG 2400
Prerequisites: Permission of instructor and GEOG 1000 or GEOG 2400

Course: GEOG 2600 — Environmental Science Data Visualization (3 credits)
Offered: every other year This course explores environmental science concepts using real data. Students develop skills in importing, exploring, cleaning and displaying data using Python programs to aid in analysis, interpretation, and communication. Topics include data importing & exploring, statistics, histograms, linefitting, moving average, and generating heat maps for hazard assessment purposes. Prerequisite(s): MATH 1300 or MATH 1700 or MATH 1800 and either CSC 1500 or GEOG 1000 Meets General Education Requirement(s): PL
Prerequisites: MATH 1300 or MATH 1700 or MATH 1800 and either CSC 1500 or GEOG 1000

Course: PHS 2900 — Global Health (Study Abroad) (3 credits)
Offered: every other year The purpose of this course is to introduce students to an overview of Global health challenges with a focus on the Healthcare system of with a focus on a health care system outside the United States. Issues in international health including public health, environmental health, global conflict, infectious disease, and chronic illness will be explored. Additionally, the course will focus on seeking and identifying solutions necessary to realize the overarching goal of health equity. Prerequisite(s): NURS 2300 or PHS 2000 or by permission of instructor.
Prerequisites: NURS 2300 or PHS 2000 or by permission of instructor.

Course: BSAD 3000 — Business System Life Cycle and Project Management (3 credits)
Offered: Spring This hands-on course provides an introduction to the fundamental concepts and various techniques and tools required to successfully manage technology projects. Topics will include the systems development life cycle, with a particular focus on the planning phase. Students will develop project planning documents using multiple tools while investigating the different management styles and tactics used throughout the project’s life cycle. Prerequisite(s): BSAD 1700 or CSC 1400
Prerequisites: BSAD 1700 or CSC 1400

Course: BSAD 4000 — Introduction to Business Analytics (3 credits)
Offered: Fall This course provides an overview of employing analytics in business. Students will examine different levels of analytics, investigate various types of business initiatives to determine which level is appropriate, and how to plan a data analytics project. Through the use of an analytics platform, the focus will include data fundamentals, statistical thinking, and communicating with data by creating and interpreting data visualizations to make business decisions. Prerequisite(s): Junior or Senior standing and has met the QR requirement.
Prerequisites: Junior or Senior standing and has met the QR requirement.

Course: EXSS 3110 — Essentials of Personal Training (3 credits)
Offered: annually The course will cover pertinent resistance training techniques and fitness assessments commonly utilized with the general public. Techniques will include exercises and assessments for overall muscular fitness. Students will have the experience to connect scientific principles with the applied skills of weight training. Prerequisite(s): EXSS 2072
Prerequisites: EXSS 2072

Course: ENGT 4930 — Engineering Technology Internship (3 credits)
Offered: Fall Students will acquire their career based professional skills and knowledge through the internship as an off-campus experiential learning activity. They have to get pre-approval from the department/university prior to involve in the internship project on-site to get internship credits. Prerequisite(s): Junior status Meets General Education Requirement(s): AIA
Prerequisites: Junior status

Course: COMM 2480 — Podcasting (3 credits)
Offered: every Spring In this course, students will conceptualize, develop, and produce an original podcast.The course will examine audio storytelling and podcast design aesthetics. Students will research an idea and target audience, and practice the craft of interviewing, audio recording, editing, mastering, and distributing the podcast. Prerequisite(s): COMM 1205 or COMM 1220
Prerequisites: COMM 1205 or COMM 1220

Course: COMM 4870 — COMM Internship (6 credits)
Offered: every Spring The 6-credit Communications Media Internship is an 18 hours per week, semester-long, pre-professional learning experience that is designed to link academic preparation with successful entry into a student’s career path. Interns must successfully complete an Internship Qualification Program in the semester immediately prior to the internship. Prerequisite(s): Completion of all Internship Preparation requirements, senior status, and a cumulative Communications Media Grade Point Average of 2.5 or better at the time the Application for Internship is due. Note: Internship placement is competitive and based on academic performance, faculty recommendations, and performance in the Internship Qualification Program.
Prerequisites: Completion of all Internship Preparation requirements, senior status, and a cumulative Communications Media Grade Point Average of 2.5 or better at the time the Application for Internship is due.

Course: DMI 1200 — Issues in Digital Media Innovation (1 credits)
Offered: Fall This course offers students the opportunity to experiment with emerging digital tools while examining their impact on society, industry, and culture. Additionally, students will investigate both the research and career opportunities related to one of more of the pathways offered as part of the Digital Media Innovation major. The course can be repeated up to three times given different course topics. The course will cover different emerging topics each time it is offered and also allow students to customize the topics they explore related to the major and associated careers. Note: This course is restricted to students in the Digital Media Innovation program.
Prerequisites: None listed

Course: GAME 3100 — 2D Game Development (3 credits)
Offered: Spring In this course, students will explore the art, design, and development of 2D games. Students will work individually and in teams to create several digital 2D games with original content. Prerequisite(s): CSC 3010 or GAME 3000
Prerequisites: CSC 3010 or GAME 3000

Course: MATH 2800 — Introduction to Statistical Analysis (3 credits)
Offered: every semester This course introduces data collection, analysis, and interpretation through descriptive statistics, regression analysis, and inferential statistics. Students will explore parameter estimation and hypothesis testing through both simulation methods and classical mathematical models. Students will write short computer programs and use statistical software to do these analyses in reproducible ways. Prerequisite(s): Grade of 1.7 or higher in MATH 1250 or MATH 1300 Note: Credit is awarded for only one of MATH 1700 , MATH 1800 , and MATH 2800. Meets General Education Requirement(s): PL
Prerequisites: Grade of 1.7 or higher in MATH 1250 or MATH 1300

Course: DATA 2000 — Principles of Data Analysis (3 credits)
Offered: Spring This course explores the process of using data to answer applied questions. Students develop skills to produce clear, thorough, and appropriate data analyses. Topics include cleaning and exploring data; building and evaluating regression, classification, and clustering models; and communicating results. Students will write short computer programs to do reproducible analyses. Prerequisite(s): MATH 1250 or MATH 1300 or MATH 1700 or MATH 1800 or MATH 2800 Meets General Education Requirement(s): PL
Prerequisites: MATH 1250 or MATH 1300 or MATH 1700 or MATH 1800 or MATH 2800

Course: MATH 4950 — Internship (1-6 credits)
Offered: as needed The Internship provides an opportunity to gain valuable on-the-job experience for university credit by working in business, government, industry or academia. Students select and apply for field placement with the assistance of the Mathematics Department and Career Services. Prerequisite(s): Junior or senior standing and approval of the Mathematics Department Note: An Internship course may be repeated for a maximum of 12 semester hours total. Only 3 internship credits may be used toward mathematics major requirements.
Prerequisites: Junior or senior standing and approval of the Mathematics Department

Course: PSY 2040 — Research Methods and Statistics 1 (3 credits)
Offered: every semester This is the first of a two course sequence in statistics and research methods. The course will give students the tools required to conduct basic descriptive research and descriptive data analysis in psychology and related fields. APA (American Psychological Association) format will be utilized. Students must earn a minimum of 2.0 in this course to progress to PSY 2050 . Prerequisite(s): A grade of 2.0 or higher in both PSY 1100 and either MATH 1700 or HON 1700
Prerequisites: A grade of 2.0 or higher in both PSY 1100 and either MATH 1700 or HON 1700

Course: PSY 2050 — Research Methods and Statistics 2 (3 credits)
Offered: every semester This is the second of a two course sequence in Research Methods and Statistics. The course will explore different experimental designs and the analysis of these designs using inferential statistics. Students must earn a minimum of 2.0 in this course to meet the major requirement. Prerequisite(s): A grade of 2.0 or higher in PSY 2040
Prerequisites: A grade of 2.0 or higher in PSY 2040

Course: PSY 3610 — Advanced Research Methods: Cognition (3 credits)
Offered: Spring This course will introduce research methods in cognitive psychology, the scientific study of how people perceive, learn, remember, and think. Students will complete simulations of experiments, analyze the data, interpret the results, and write lab reports. Prerequisite(s): PSY 2040 or PSY 2000 or by permission of instructor Pre/Concurrent: PSY 3550
Prerequisites: PSY 2040 or PSY 2000 or by permission of instructor

Course: PSY 3620 — Advanced Research Methods: Learning (3 credits)
Offered: Fall In this advanced research methods course, students will explore the principles and procedures of single-subject research in classical and operant conditioning by conducting virtual behavior change experiments. Real-world applications will be considered. This course will be taken concurrently with or after completion of Psychology of Learning. Prerequisite(s): PSY 2040 or PSY 2000 or by permission of instructor Pre/Concurrent: PSY 3500
Prerequisites: PSY 2040 or PSY 2000 or by permission of instructor

Course: PSY 3630 — Advanced Research Methods: Sensation and Perception (3 credits)
Offered: Fall This advanced research methods course serves as an introduction to research methods in cognitive science, with a specific focus on Sensation and Perception. Students will complete sensation lab exercises, design, conduct, and analyze experiments, and write up results in lab reports. Prerequisite(s): PSY 2040 or PSY 2000 or by permission of instructor Pre/Concurrent: PSY 3430
Prerequisites: PSY 2040 or PSY 2000 or by permission of instructor

Course: PSY 3640 — Advanced Research Methods: Motivation (3 credits)
Offered: Spring In this advanced research methods course, students will actively explore the principles and procedures of motivational science. Students will design, conduct, and analyze biological, learned, and cognitive components of motivation with the goal of writing a complete APA-style lab report. Prerequisite(s): PSY 2040 or PSY 2000 or by permission of instructor Pre/Concurrent: PSY 3530
Prerequisites: PSY 2040 or PSY 2000 or by permission of instructor

Course: HMSV 3450 — Aging Social Welfare Policy (3 credits)
Offered: every spring semester An introduction to the history of social policies and policymaking addressing needs of older adults and their families. Students learn skills in critical policy analysis, by examining individual experiences of aging (and impacts of sociocultural diversity), family and community responses to aging needs, and social policies to address those needs. *Field Experience: This course requires the successful completion of a 45-hour supervised experience in a elder services agency. Students must complete the practicum in order to receive a passing grade for the course. Prerequisite(s): HMSV 1100
Prerequisites: HMSV 1100

Course: HMSV 3550 — Mental and Behavioral Health Policy (3 credits)
Offered: once per year This course is an introduction to the history and trajectory of mental and behavioral health policies in the United States. Students learn skills in analysis and application of mental and behavioral health policies and systems of care from a prevention, intervention, culturally-informed framework. This course focuses on the presentation, risk and correlated factors, treatment, advocacy, and family and social factors impacting behaviors and mental health from a broad, strengths-based perspective. Students will examine how current US policy impacts or impedes wellness from a mental and behavioral health perspective, particularly from a social and cultural identity standpoint. Students will also compare policy across the US and other countries. *Field Experience: This course requires the successful completion of a 45 hour supervised experience in a mental or behavioral health program or agency. Students must complete the practicum in order to receive a passing grade for the course. Prerequisite(s): HMSV 1100 and PSY 1100
Prerequisites: HMSV 1100 and PSY 1100

Course: HMSV 3610 — Assessment in Human Services (3 credits)
Offered: every semester This core course focuses on the process of assessment in a variety of human service settings. It is designed to provide the knowledge and practical skills necessary to collect, organize, interpret, analyze, and report on information related to clients’ needs, functioning and progress; to establish and monitor service plans; to broker services; and to evaluate service provision. Examples of assessment in schools, courts, hospitals, clinics, and other human service agencies will be provided. Students will focus on understanding and applying the assessment process through a cultural and social identity sensitive lens. Prerequisite(s): HMSV 1100 , HMSV 2500 , and PSY 1200 Note: A 2.5 is required in this course to use toward Human Services major. Corequisite(s): HMSV 3620
Prerequisites: HMSV 1100 , HMSV 2500 , and PSY 1200

Course: HMSV 3620 — Intervention in Human Services (3 credits)
Offered: every semester This course expands on Interviewing Techniques and supports learning in Assessment by providing practical experience in understanding and implementing intervention strategies. A special focus will be given to working with individuals and families, drawing from problem management approaches and addressing motivation-to-change issues in a generalist practice setting. Prerequisite(s): HMSV 1100 , HMSV 2500 , and PSY 1200 Corequisite(s): HMSV 3610
Prerequisites: HMSV 1100 , HMSV 2500 , and PSY 1200

Course: MUSC 1351 — Applied Piano Lessons Level 1 (1 credits)
Offered: Offered every semester. This course provides private study of piano that includes the development of technique, repertoire, and performance practice related to piano performance. These lessons occur in a one-on-one studio setting and are scheduled by the student in conjunction with the Humanities Department and the applied music instructor. A jury performance is required and opportunities for student recital performance may be available. Seven hours of individual instruction per semester. Note: May be repeated for a maximum of two credits. Meets General Education Requirement(s): FA
Prerequisites: None listed

Course: PSY 3650 — Advanced Research Methods: Social Psychology (3 credits)
Offered: Fall This advanced research methods course will introduce research methods in the field of Social Psychology. Students will complete lab exercises, design, conduct, and analyze experiments, and write up results in lab reports. Prerequisite(s): PSY 2040 or by permission of the instructor Pre/Concurrent: PSY 2500
Prerequisites: PSY 2040 or by permission of the instructor

Course: COMM 3440 — Documentary Development (3 credits)
Offered: every Spring Students will engage in a hands-on study of the methods and practices involved in the research and development of documentary films and other non-fiction media, across a variety of representational styles and formats. Students will uncover a topic of interest and immerse in the conception, planning, research, and writing stages that precede the production phase of non-fiction filmmaking and other forms of the genre. Prerequisite(s): ENGL 1200 or HON 1200 and Junior or Senior standing. Meets General Education Requirement(s): AIA, IHIP
Prerequisites: ENGL 1200 or HON 1200 and Junior or Senior standing.

Course: IDIS 1250 — Introduction to Sustainability Studies (3 credits)
Prerequisites: None listed

Course: ART 2215 — A Cultural History of Nature (3 credits)
Prerequisites: None listed

Course: SPCH 2200 — Environmental Communication (3 credits)
Offered: every other year This course explores the communication and public forums that are used to address environmental controversies. Students will be introduced to the transdisciplinary field of environmental communication and to a variety of perspectives that complement its study. Prerequisite(s): ENGL 1200 or HON 1200 Meets General Education Requirement(s): AIA, CV, IHIP
Prerequisites: ENGL 1200 or HON 1200

Course: SOC 2025 — Environmental Sociology (3 credits)
Offered: Spring This course examines the role of power, inequality, and culture in shaping environmental outcomes, and emphasizes the importance of environmental justice. It challenges students to reflect on the complacency of contemporary life and its environmental consequences. Prerequisite(s): IDIS 1250 or SOC 1100 or permission from the instructor Meets General Education Requirement(s): CV, DP
Prerequisites: IDIS 1250 or SOC 1100 or permission from the instructor

Course: COMM 2465 — Introduction to Video Editing (3 credits)
Offered: every Fall Intro to Video Editing is an introductory-level workshop course that explores the editing and delivery of short non-fiction video content. Editing and motion graphics software will be utilized in the production of works such as human-interest profile, news reportage, and promotional content. Basic motion graphics and audio mixing will be integrated in finished work. Prerequisite(s): Sophomore standing and either COMM 1205 or COMM 1220
Prerequisites: Sophomore standing and either COMM 1205 or COMM 1220

Course: COMM 3680 — Photography Portfolio Development (3 credits)
Offered: every Spring Photography Portfolio Development allows upper-level students to develop a highly focused and organized portfolio suitable for professional and artistic domains. In addition, the course looks at promotional strategies for a photography career, engaging students in developing components ranging from business plans, niche brand identity, logos, and websites to collateral and professional resumes specific to a professional career in Photography. Prerequisite(s): COMM 3620 (may be taken concurrently) or permission of instructor.
Prerequisites: COMM 3620 (may be taken concurrently) or permission of instructor.

Course: SOC 3050 — Social Research Methods (3 credits)
Offered: Spring This course equips students with skills in quantitative and qualitative research methods. Students will learn how to design and apply methods such as surveys, interviews, and content analysis to investigate social issues, while also gaining handson experience in literature reviews, data collection, and data interpretation. Prerequisite(s): SOC 1100 and one from MATH 1100 , MATH 1700 , MATH 1800 . Sophomore, junior, or senior status. Meets General Education Requirement(s): AIA, IHIP
Prerequisites: SOC 1100 and one from MATH 1100 , MATH 1700 , MATH 1800 . Sophomore, junior, or senior status.
